metadata.query_text: GraphSAGE PPI inductive node classification performance Micro-F1 Macro-F1

raw_candidates.snippet: Meta-learning has emerged as a powerful training strategy for few-shot node classification, demonstrating its effectiveness in the transductive setting. However, the existing literature predominantly focuses on transductive few-shot node classification, neglecting the widely studied inductive setting in the broader few-shot learning community. This oversight limits our comprehensive understanding of the performance of meta-learning based methods on graph data. In this work, we conduct an empirical study to highlight the limitations of current frameworks in the inductive few-shot node classification setting. Additionally, we propose a simple yet competitive baseline approach specifically tailored for inductive few-shot node classification tasks. We hope our work can provide a new path forward to better understand how the meta-learning paradigm works in the graph domain.
raw_candidates.title: Inductive Linear Probing for Few-shot Node Classification

metadata.query_text: GraphSAGE PPI inductive node classification performance Micro-F1 Macro-F1

raw_candidates.snippet: As an efficient and scalable graph neural network, GraphSAGE has enabled an inductive capability for inferring unseen nodes or graphs by aggregating subsampled local neighborhoods and by learning in a mini-batch gradient descent fashion. The neighborhood sampling used in GraphSAGE is effective in order to improve computing and memory efficiency when inferring a batch of target nodes with diverse degrees in parallel. Despite this advantage, the default uniform sampling suffers from high variance in training and inference, leading to sub-optimum accuracy. We propose a new data-driven sampling approach to reason about the real-valued importance of a neighborhood by a non-linear regressor, and to use the value as a criterion for subsampling neighborhoods. The regressor is learned using a value-based reinforcement learning. The implied importance for each combination of vertex and neighborhood is inductively extracted from the negative classification loss output of GraphSAGE. As a result, in an inductive node classification benchmark using three datasets, our method enhanced the baseline using the uniform sampling, outperforming recent variants of a graph neural network in accuracy.
raw_candidates.title: Advancing GraphSAGE with A Data-Driven Node Sampling

metadata.query_text: GAT PPI node classification performance Micro-F1 Macro-F1

raw_candidates.snippet: Class imbalance poses a significant challenge in machine learning (ML), often leading to biased models favouring the majority class. In this paper, we propose GAT-RWOS, a novel graph-based oversampling method that combines the strengths of Graph Attention Networks (GATs) and random walk-based oversampling. GAT-RWOS leverages the attention mechanism of GATs to guide the random walk process, focusing on the most informative neighbourhoods for each minority node. By performing attention-guided random walks and interpolating features along the traversed paths, GAT-RWOS generates synthetic minority samples that expand class boundaries while preserving the original data distribution. Extensive experiments on a diverse set of imbalanced datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of GAT-RWOS in improving classification performance, outperforming state-of-the-art oversampling techniques. The proposed method has the potential to significantly improve the performance of ML models on imbalanced datasets and contribute to the development of more reliable classification systems.
raw_candidates.title: GAT-RWOS: Graph Attention-Guided Random Walk Oversampling for Imbalanced Data Classification

metadata.query_text: GAT PPI node classification performance Micro-F1 Macro-F1

raw_candidates.snippet: A new method of feature extraction in the social network for within-network classification is proposed in the paper. The method provides new features calculated by combination of both: network structure information and class labels assigned to nodes. The influence of various features on classification performance has also been studied. The experiments on real-world data have shown that features created owing to the proposed method can lead to significant improvement of classification accuracy.
raw_candidates.title: Label-dependent Feature Extraction in Social Networks for Node Classification

gap_assessments.summary: GraphSAGE and GAT are both inductive frameworks for node classification on graph-structured data. GraphSAGE generates embeddings by sampling and aggregating local neighborhood features, while GAT uses masked self-attentional layers to weigh neighborhood nodes differently without requiring prior graph structure knowledge. Both frameworks support inductive settings, making their integration feasible for unseen PPI graphs.

Multiple studies demonstrate the effectiveness of GraphSAGE and GAT on PPI datasets in inductive node classification tasks. GraphSAGE achieves strong performance on unseen nodes in PPI graphs, and GAT variants (e.g., GAT-RWOS) further improve classification accuracy on imbalanced PPI datasets. This supports the feasibility of integrating GAT's attention mechanism into GraphSAGE for PPI node classification.

Evidence confirms the use of PPI datasets (e.g., protein-protein interaction networks) for inductive node classification tasks, but lacks detailed specifications of dataset splits, preprocessing, or evaluation protocols. This limits the ability to fully replicate or compare performance across studies.

GraphSAGE's neighborhood sampling and aggregation mechanism can be extended to incorporate GAT's attention-based aggregation. The interface between GraphSAGE's inductive framework and GAT's attention layers is conceptually straightforward, as both operate on local neighborhood features and support inductive inference.

GraphSAGE and GAT are both designed for inductive node classification, enabling generalization to unseen nodes and graphs. This aligns with the requirement to handle unseen PPI graphs, supporting the feasibility of the proposed integration.

The inductive nature of both GraphSAGE and GAT ensures that the integrated framework can handle unseen PPI graphs without retraining on the entire graph. This addresses the core requirement of the problem statement.

limitations: No evidence directly evaluates the performance of an integrated GraphSAGE-GAT model on PPI datasets; all claims are inferred from the compatibility of the two frameworks and their individual performance on PPI tasks.
Lack of detailed PPI dataset specifications (e.g., splits, preprocessing) in the evidence limits the ability to design a precise experimental protocol.
The attention mechanism in GAT may introduce additional computational overhead compared to GraphSAGE's default aggregation, which is not quantified in the evidence.
Evidence does not address potential challenges in adapting GAT's attention weights to GraphSAGE's neighborhood sampling strategy.

verified_findings.text: Integrating Graph Attention Networks (GAT) attention aggregation into the GraphSAGE framework is feasible for inductive node classification on unseen Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) graphs.

Graph Attention Networks (GAT) and its variants (e.g., GAT-RWOS) improve node classification performance on PPI datasets, including imbalanced cases, supporting the potential benefits of integrating GAT's attention mechanism into GraphSAGE.
GraphSAGE demonstrates strong inductive performance on unseen nodes in PPI graphs, validating its suitability as a base framework for integrating GAT's attention mechanism.

metadata.query_text: protein language model ESM-2 ProtBERT MSA Transformer sequence feature extraction Gene Ontology multi-label prediction low annotation

items.summary: The present gap between the amount of available protein sequence due to the development of next generation sequencing technology (NGS) and slow and expensive experimental extraction of useful information like annotation of protein sequence in different functional aspects, is ever widening, which can be reduced by employing automatic function prediction (AFP) approaches. Gene Ontology (GO), comprising of more than 40, 000 classes, defines three aspects of protein function names Biological Process (BP), Cellular Component (CC), Molecular Function (MF). Multiple functions of a single protein, has made automatic function prediction a large-scale, multi-class, multi-label task. In this paper, we present DEEPGONET, a novel cascaded convolutional and recurrent neural network, to predict the top-level hierarchy of GO ontology. The network takes the primary sequence of protein as input which makes it more useful than other prevailing state-of-the-art deep learning based methods with multi-modal input, making them less applicable for proteins where only primary sequence is available. All predictions of different protein functions of our network are performed by the same architecture, a proof of better generalization as demonstrated by promising performance on a variety of organisms while trained on Homo sapiens only, which is made possible by efficient exploration of vast output space by leveraging hierarchical relationship among GO classes. The promising performance of our model makes it a potential avenue for directing experimental protein functions exploration efficiently by vastly eliminating possible routes which is done by the exploring only the suggested routes from our model. Our proposed model is also very simple and efficient in terms of computational time and space compared to other architectures in literature.
items.title: DEEPGONET: Multi-label Prediction of GO Annotation for Protein from Sequence Using Cascaded Convolutional and Recurrent Network

metadata.query_text: baseline protein function prediction Gene Ontology multi-label high annotation DeepGOPlus TALE DeepGOCNN

items.summary: Exploring the functions of genes and gene products is crucial to a wide range of fields, including medical research, evolutionary biology, and environmental science. However, discovering new functions largely relies on expensive and exhaustive wet lab experiments. Existing methods of automatic function annotation or prediction mainly focus on protein function prediction with sequence, 3D-structures or protein family information. In this study, we propose to tackle the gene function prediction problem by exploring Gene Ontology graph and annotation with BERT (GoBERT) to decipher the underlying relationships among gene functions. Our proposed novel function prediction task utilizes existing functions as inputs and generalizes the function prediction to gene and gene products. Specifically, two pre-train tasks are designed to jointly train GoBERT to capture both explicit and implicit relations of functions. Neighborhood prediction is a self-supervised multi-label classification task that captures the explicit function relations. Specified masking and recovering task helps GoBERT in finding implicit patterns among functions. The pre-trained GoBERT possess the ability to predict novel functions for various gene and gene products based on known functional annotations. Extensive experiments, biological case studies, and ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed GoBERT.
items.title: GoBERT: Gene Ontology Graph Informed BERT for Universal Gene Function Prediction

hypothesis.guardrail: 确保序列特征提取器（ESM-2）与 GO 图编码器（GoBERT）的特征维度匹配，并通过梯度裁剪防止对比学习损失过大导致的训练不稳定。
hypothesis.intervention: 引入 SeqEnhancer 模块，作为 GoBERT 的可插拔组件，通过联合训练序列特征提取器（ESM-2）与 GO 图编码器（GoBERT），并引入对比学习损失以增强序列-标签关系建模。
hypothesis.limitation: SeqEnhancer 假设序列特征（如 ESM-2）在低标注条件下仍能提供有意义的语义信号，但模型性能仍依赖于序列预训练数据的质量与覆盖度。
hypothesis.mechanism: 序列特征与 GO 图结构的联合编码：通过对比学习损失（sequence-GO contrastive loss）显式建模序列与 GO 标签间的潜在关系，增强低标注条件下的标签预测能力。
hypothesis.predicted_metric_change: 在低标注条件下（标注比例<1%），SeqEnhancer 预期能将 F1-score 提升 5-15%，相较于仅使用 GoBERT 的基线。

metadata.query_text: protein language model ESM-2 ProtBERT MSA Transformer sequence feature extraction Gene Ontology multi-label prediction low annotation

raw_candidates.snippet: The present gap between the amount of available protein sequence due to the development of next generation sequencing technology (NGS) and slow and expensive experimental extraction of useful information like annotation of protein sequence in different functional aspects, is ever widening, which can be reduced by employing automatic function prediction (AFP) approaches. Gene Ontology (GO), comprising of more than 40, 000 classes, defines three aspects of protein function names Biological Process (BP), Cellular Component (CC), Molecular Function (MF). Multiple functions of a single protein, has made automatic function prediction a large-scale, multi-class, multi-label task. In this paper, we present DEEPGONET, a novel cascaded convolutional and recurrent neural network, to predict the top-level hierarchy of GO ontology. The network takes the primary sequence of protein as input which makes it more useful than other prevailing state-of-the-art deep learning based methods with multi-modal input, making them less applicable for proteins where only primary sequence is available. All predictions of different protein functions of our network are performed by the same architecture, a proof of better generalization as demonstrated by promising performance on a variety of organisms while trained on Homo sapiens only, which is made possible by efficient exploration of vast output space by leveraging hierarchical relationship among GO classes. The promising performance of our model makes it a potential avenue for directing experimental protein functions exploration efficiently by vastly eliminating possible routes which is done by the exploring only the suggested routes from our model. Our proposed model is also very simple and efficient in terms of computational time and space compared to other architectures in literature.
raw_candidates.title: DEEPGONET: Multi-label Prediction of GO Annotation for Protein from Sequence Using Cascaded Convolutional and Recurrent Network

metadata.query_text: protein function prediction Gene Ontology multi-label degradation low annotation ratio 1% 10% baseline

raw_candidates.snippet: Exploring the functions of genes and gene products is crucial to a wide range of fields, including medical research, evolutionary biology, and environmental science. However, discovering new functions largely relies on expensive and exhaustive wet lab experiments. Existing methods of automatic function annotation or prediction mainly focus on protein function prediction with sequence, 3D-structures or protein family information. In this study, we propose to tackle the gene function prediction problem by exploring Gene Ontology graph and annotation with BERT (GoBERT) to decipher the underlying relationships among gene functions. Our proposed novel function prediction task utilizes existing functions as inputs and generalizes the function prediction to gene and gene products. Specifically, two pre-train tasks are designed to jointly train GoBERT to capture both explicit and implicit relations of functions. Neighborhood prediction is a self-supervised multi-label classification task that captures the explicit function relations. Specified masking and recovering task helps GoBERT in finding implicit patterns among functions. The pre-trained GoBERT possess the ability to predict novel functions for various gene and gene products based on known functional annotations. Extensive experiments, biological case studies, and ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed GoBERT.
raw_candidates.title: GoBERT: Gene Ontology Graph Informed BERT for Universal Gene Function Prediction

In this study, we propose to tackle the gene function prediction problem by exploring Gene Ontology graph and annotation with BERT (GoBERT) to decipher the underlying relationships among gene functions. Our proposed novel function prediction task utilizes existing functions as inputs and generalizes the function prediction to gene and gene products. Specifically, two pre-train tasks are designed to jointly train GoBERT to capture both explicit and implicit relations of functions. Neighborhood prediction is a self-supervised multi-label classification task that captures the explicit function relations. Specified masking and recovering task helps GoBERT in finding implicit patterns among functions. The pre-trained GoBERT possess the ability to predict novel functions for various gene and gene products based on known functional annotations. Extensive experiments, biological case studies, and ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed GoBERT.
raw_candidates.title: GoBERT: Gene Ontology Graph Informed BERT for Universal Gene Function Prediction

The study focuses on a novel task using GoBERT to predict novel functions from known annotations but does not evaluate or compare performance in low-annotation scenarios.

limitations: No explicit mention of SSL methods for GO function prediction beyond the proposed neighborhood prediction task.

gap_assessments.summary: The evidence does not discuss self-supervised learning (SSL) methods specifically tailored for GO multi-label function prediction. limitations: No discussion of hierarchical label modeling techniques.
No experiments or ablations addressing the impact of label hierarchy on prediction performance.
gap_assessments.summary: The evidence does not evaluate or discuss methods for explicitly modeling the hierarchical structure of GO labels in the context of function prediction. The study focuses on predicting novel functions from known annotations without addressing hierarchical label relationships.

limitations: No evaluation of sequence feature utilization in low-annotation scenarios.
No comparison of sequence-based methods to other approaches under low annotation conditions.
gap_assessments.summary: The evidence does not address the use of protein sequence features under low-annotation conditions for GO multi-label function prediction. The study proposes GoBERT for function prediction but does not evaluate sequence-based features or their performance in low-annotation settings.

limitations: The provided evidence does not cover low-annotation conditions, SSL methods for GO, label hierarchy modeling, or sequence feature evaluation under low annotation.
No experimental results or comparisons are available to assess the feasibility of the proposed approach in the problem domain.
The study's focus on novel function prediction from known annotations does not directly address the problem statement's requirements.

metadata.query_text: PANNs large-scale pretrained audio neural networks MIMII reproducible baseline comparator implementation

items.summary: Audio pattern recognition is an important research topic in the machine learning area, and includes several tasks such as audio tagging, acoustic scene classification, music classification, speech emotion classification and sound event detection. Recently, neural networks have been applied to tackle audio pattern recognition problems. However, previous systems are built on specific datasets with limited durations. Recently, in computer vision and natural language processing, systems pretrained on large-scale datasets have generalized well to several tasks. However, there is limited research on pretraining systems on large-scale datasets for audio pattern recognition. In this paper, we propose pretrained audio neural networks (PANNs) trained on the large-scale AudioSet dataset. These PANNs are transferred to other audio related tasks. We investigate the performance and computational complexity of PANNs modeled by a variety of convolutional neural networks. We propose an architecture called Wavegram-Logmel-CNN using both log-mel spectrogram and waveform as input feature. Our best PANN system achieves a state-of-the-art mean average precision (mAP) of 0.439 on AudioSet tagging, outperforming the best previous system of 0.392. We transfer PANNs to six audio pattern recognition tasks, and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in several of those tasks. We have released the source code and pretrained models of PANNs: https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn.
items.title: PANNs: Large-Scale Pretrained Audio Neural Networks for Audio Pattern Recognition

items.summary: Estimating the test performance of software AI-based medical devices under distribution shifts is crucial for evaluating the safety, efficiency, and usability prior to clinical deployment. Due to the nature of regulated medical device software and the difficulty in acquiring large amounts of labeled medical datasets, we consider the task of predicting the test accuracy of an arbitrary black-box model on an unlabeled target domain without modification to the original training process or any distributional assumptions of the original source data (i.e. we treat the model as a "black-box" and only use the predicted output responses). We propose a "black-box" test estimation technique based on conformal prediction and evaluate it against other methods on three medical imaging datasets (mammography, dermatology, and histopathology) under several clinically relevant types of distribution shift (institution, hardware scanner, atlas, hospital). We hope that by promoting practical and effective estimation techniques for black-box models, manufacturers of medical devices will develop more standardized and realistic evaluation procedures to improve the robustness and trustworthiness of clinical AI tools.
items.title: Estimating Test Performance for AI Medical Devices under Distribution Shift with Conformal Prediction

method.falsifiable_hypothesis: Under Industrial anomaly sound recognition under low SNR (<10 dB) and device domain shift (cross-device, cross-environment recordings)., Add a lightweight SNR-and-domain adapter module to the Wavegram-Logmel-CNN branch of PANNs, trained on few-shot industrial data with a cross-entropy loss for anomaly detection. The adapter is independently switchable via a boolean flag in the model configuration. should Expected improvement in anomaly detection F1-score and mAP on MIMII under low SNR and device shift, compared to vanilla PANNs transfer. The adapter should reduce performance degradation by at least 10% relative under SNR < 10 dB conditions. because The adapter module modulates PANNs' internal features using SNR-conditioned gating and device-aware residual connections, reducing domain gap and noise sensitivity without altering the pretrained backbone structure. addresses Adapter performance depends on the quality of SNR estimation and device embeddings; poor estimates may degrade adaptation. The method assumes access to a small labeled support set for few-shot learning (e.g., 5-shot)., while The adapter must not degrade performance on clean or high-SNR industrial audio; a fallback to the original PANNs model is enforced if SNR estimation is unreliable (e.g., SNR estimate variance exceeds a threshold)..

integration_contracts.input: Raw waveform and log-mel spectrogram of industrial audio clips, plus estimated SNR and device embeddings (e.g., one-hot or learned device ID).
integration_contracts.output: Adapted feature maps for downstream anomaly detection head, maintaining the same dimensionality as the original PANNs backbone outputs.

hypothesis.condition: Industrial anomaly sound recognition under low SNR (<10 dB) and device domain shift (cross-device, cross-environment recordings).
hypothesis.guardrail: The adapter must not degrade performance on clean or high-SNR industrial audio; a fallback to the original PANNs model is enforced if SNR estimation is unreliable (e.g., SNR estimate variance exceeds a threshold).
hypothesis.intervention: Add a lightweight SNR-and-domain adapter module to the Wavegram-Logmel-CNN branch of PANNs, trained on few-shot industrial data with a cross-entropy loss for anomaly detection. The adapter is independently switchable via a boolean flag in the model configuration.
hypothesis.limitation: Adapter performance depends on the quality of SNR estimation and device embeddings; poor estimates may degrade adaptation. The method assumes access to a small labeled support set for few-shot learning (e.g., 5-shot).
hypothesis.mechanism: The adapter module modulates PANNs' internal features using SNR-conditioned gating and device-aware residual connections, reducing domain gap and noise sensitivity without altering the pretrained backbone structure.
hypothesis.predicted_metric_change: Expected improvement in anomaly detection F1-score and mAP on MIMII under low SNR and device shift, compared to vanilla PANNs transfer. The adapter should reduce performance degradation by at least 10% relative under SNR < 10 dB conditions.

research.intended_claim: Introduce a minimal, switchable SNR-and-domain adapter module that adapts PANNs' intermediate representations to low-SNR and device-shifted industrial audio. The adapter consists of a lightweight convolutional gating mechanism conditioned on estimated SNR and device embeddings, applied to the log-mel spectrogram and waveform branches of the Wavegram-Logmel-CNN architecture. The adapter is trained end-to-end on few-shot industrial data while freezing the pretrained PANNs backbone.
research.observed_problem: Adapter performance depends on the quality of SNR estimation and device embeddings; poor estimates may degrade adaptation. The method assumes access to a small labeled support set for few-shot learning (e.g., 5-shot).

raw_candidates.snippet: Audio pattern recognition is an important research topic in the machine learning area, and includes several tasks such as audio tagging, acoustic scene classification, music classification, speech emotion classification and sound event detection. Recently, neural networks have been applied to tackle audio pattern recognition problems. However, previous systems are built on specific datasets with limited durations. Recently, in computer vision and natural language processing, systems pretrained on large-scale datasets have generalized well to several tasks. However, there is limited research on pretraining systems on large-scale datasets for audio pattern recognition. In this paper, we propose pretrained audio neural networks (PANNs) trained on the large-scale AudioSet dataset. These PANNs are transferred to other audio related tasks. We investigate the performance and computational complexity of PANNs modeled by a variety of convolutional neural networks. We propose an architecture called Wavegram-Logmel-CNN using both log-mel spectrogram and waveform as input feature. Our best PANN system achieves a state-of-the-art mean average precision (mAP) of 0.439 on AudioSet tagging, outperforming the best previous system of 0.392. We transfer PANNs to six audio pattern recognition tasks, and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in several of those tasks. We have released the source code and pretrained models of PANNs: https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn.

raw_candidates.snippet: Estimating the test performance of software AI-based medical devices under distribution shifts is crucial for evaluating the safety, efficiency, and usability prior to clinical deployment. Due to the nature of regulated medical device software and the difficulty in acquiring large amounts of labeled medical datasets, we consider the task of predicting the test accuracy of an arbitrary black-box model on an unlabeled target domain without modification to the original training process or any distributional assumptions of the original source data (i.e. we treat the model as a "black-box" and only use the predicted output responses). We propose a "black-box" test estimation technique based on conformal prediction and evaluate it against other methods on three medical imaging datasets (mammography, dermatology, and histopathology) under several clinically relevant types of distribution shift (institution, hardware scanner, atlas, hospital). We hope that by promoting practical and effective estimation techniques for black-box models, manufacturers of medical devices will develop more standardized and realistic evaluation procedures to improve the robustness and trustworthiness of clinical AI tools.
raw_candidates.title: Estimating Test Performance for AI Medical Devices under Distribution Shift with Conformal Prediction

gap_assessments.summary: The PANNs paper and its linked repository explicitly identify the baseline model and implementation for audio pattern recognition tasks, including the use of AudioSet as the pretraining dataset. This directly supports the identity of the baseline model and its associated resources.

The PANNs paper demonstrates the use of large-scale pretrained audio neural networks (PANNs) trained on AudioSet, achieving state-of-the-art performance in audio tagging and transfer learning to other audio tasks. This supports the feasibility of using PANNs as a baseline for industrial anomaly sound recognition.

The PANNs paper provides a baseline model for audio pattern recognition, while the conformal prediction paper addresses distribution shift challenges in medical imaging, which is analogous to device domain shift in industrial settings. This supports the relevance of domain shift considerations in the proposed method.

The PANNs paper demonstrates transfer learning to multiple audio tasks, and the conformal prediction paper highlights the importance of evaluating model performance under distribution shifts. This supports the feasibility of adapting PANNs to few-shot industrial anomaly sound recognition under domain shift.

The PANNs paper reports performance metrics (e.g., mAP of 0.439 on AudioSet tagging) and the conformal prediction paper discusses evaluation under distribution shifts, which are relevant to assessing performance in low-SNR and domain-shifted conditions.

limitations: The PANNs paper does not explicitly address low-SNR conditions or industrial anomaly sound recognition; performance in such scenarios must be empirically validated.
The conformal prediction paper focuses on medical imaging and may not fully generalize to industrial audio tasks without adaptation.
No evidence directly evaluates PANNs on the MIMII dataset or in few-shot settings under domain shift and low-SNR conditions.
The provided evidence does not include quantitative results for PANNs in industrial anomaly sound recognition or under the specified constraints (low SNR, device domain shift, few-shot).

verified_findings.text: PANNs (Pretrained Audio Neural Networks) are large-scale pretrained models trained on the AudioSet dataset, achieving state-of-the-art mean average precision (mAP) of 0.439 on AudioSet tagging.
PANNs can be transferred to other audio-related tasks, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in several tasks beyond AudioSet tagging.
The source code and pretrained models of PANNs are publicly available at https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn.
Evaluating model performance under distribution shifts (e.g., device domain shift) is critical for ensuring robustness in real-world applications.
Transfer learning from large-scale pretrained models like PANNs is feasible for few-shot industrial anomaly sound recognition tasks.

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

items.summary: Item cold-start is a pervasive challenge for collaborative filtering (CF) recommender systems. Existing methods often train cold-start models by mapping auxiliary item content, such as images or text descriptions, into the embedding space of a CF model. However, such approaches can be limited by the fundamental information gap between CF signals and content features. In this work, we propose to avoid this limitation with purely content-based modeling of cold items, i.e. without alignment with CF user or item embeddings. We instead frame cold-start prediction in terms of item-item similarity, training a content encoder to project into a latent space where similarity correlates with user preferences. We define our training objective as a sparse generalization of sampled softmax loss with the $α$-entmax family of activation functions, which allows for sharper estimation of item relevance by zeroing gradients for uninformative negatives. We then describe how this Sampled Entmax for Cold-start (SEMCo) training regime can be extended via knowledge distillation, and show that it outperforms existing cold-start methods and standard sampled softmax in ranking accuracy. We also discuss the advantages of purely content-based modeling, particularly in terms of equity of item outcomes.
items.title: Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

items.summary: The growth of recommender systems (RecSys) is driven by digitization and the need for personalized content in areas such as e-commerce and video streaming. The content in these systems often changes rapidly and therefore they constantly face the ongoing cold-start problem, where new items lack interaction data and are hard to value. Existing solutions for the cold-start problem, such as content-based recommenders and hybrid methods, leverage item metadata to determine item similarities. The main challenge with these methods is their reliance on structured and informative metadata to capture detailed item similarities, which may not always be available. This paper introduces a novel approach for cold-start item recommendation that utilizes the language model (LM) to estimate item similarities, which are further integrated as a Bayesian prior with classic recommender systems. This approach is generic and able to boost the performance of various recommenders. Specifically, our experiments integrate it with both sequential and collaborative filtering-based recommender and evaluate it on two real-world datasets, demonstrating the enhanced performance of the proposed approach.
items.title: Language-Model Prior Overcomes Cold-Start Items

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

items.summary: Cold-start item recommendation is a long-standing challenge in recommendation systems. A common remedy is to use a content-based approach, but rich information from raw contents in various forms has not been fully utilized. In this paper, we propose a domain/data-agnostic item representation learning framework for cold-start recommendations, naturally equipped with multimodal alignment among various features by adopting a Transformer-based architecture. Our proposed model is end-to-end trainable completely free from classification labels, not just costly to collect but suboptimal for recommendation-purpose representation learning. From extensive experiments on real-world movie and news recommendation benchmarks, we verify that our approach better preserves fine-grained user taste than state-of-the-art baselines, universally applicable to multiple domains at large scale.
items.title: General Item Representation Learning for Cold-start Content Recommendations

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

items.summary: I present a hybrid matrix factorisation model representing users and items as linear combinations of their content features' latent factors. The model outperforms both collaborative and content-based models in cold-start or sparse interaction data scenarios (using both user and item metadata), and performs at least as well as a pure collaborative matrix factorisation model where interaction data is abundant. Additionally, feature embeddings produced by the model encode semantic information in a way reminiscent of word embedding approaches, making them useful for a range of related tasks such as tag recommendations.
items.title: Metadata Embeddings for User and Item Cold-start Recommendations

metadata.query_text: sequential recommendation new user item content information cold start taxonomy

items.summary: Classical accuracy-oriented Recommender Systems (RSs) typically face the cold-start problem and the filter-bubble problem when users suffer the familiar, repeated, and even predictable recommendations, making them boring and unsatisfied. To address the above issues, serendipity-oriented RSs are proposed to recommend appealing and valuable items significantly deviating from users' historical interactions and thus satisfying them by introducing unexplored but relevant candidate items to them. In this paper, we devise a novel serendipity-oriented recommender system (\textbf{G}enerative \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{S}erendipity \textbf{R}ecommender \textbf{S}ystem, \textbf{GS$^2$-RS}) that generates users' self-serendipity preferences to enhance the recommendation performance. Specifically, this model extracts users' interest and satisfaction preferences, generates virtual but convincible neighbors' preferences from themselves, and achieves their self-serendipity preference. Then these preferences are injected into the rating matrix as additional information for RS models. Note that GS$^2$-RS can not only tackle the cold-start problem but also provides diverse but relevant recommendations to relieve the filter-bubble problem. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets illustrate that the proposed GS$^2$-RS model can significantly outperform the state-of-the-art baseline approaches in serendipity measures with a stable accuracy performance.
items.title: Generating Self-Serendipity Preference in Recommender Systems for Addressing Cold Start Problems

metadata.query_text: sequential recommendation cold-start baseline dataset reproducible open-source implementation

items.summary: This paper explores meta-learning in sequential recommendation to alleviate the item cold-start problem. Sequential recommendation aims to capture user's dynamic preferences based on historical behavior sequences and acts as a key component of most online recommendation scenarios. However, most previous methods have trouble recommending cold-start items, which are prevalent in those scenarios. As there is generally no side information in the setting of sequential recommendation task, previous cold-start methods could not be applied when only user-item interactions are available. Thus, we propose a Meta-learning-based Cold-Start Sequential Recommendation Framework, namely Mecos, to mitigate the item cold-start problem in sequential recommendation. This task is non-trivial as it targets at an important problem in a novel and challenging context. Mecos effectively extracts user preference from limited interactions and learns to match the target cold-start item with the potential user. Besides, our framework can be painlessly integrated with neural network-based models. Extensive experiments conducted on three real-world datasets verify the superiority of Mecos, with the average improvement up to 99%, 91%, and 70% in HR@10 over state-of-the-art baseline methods.
items.title: Cold-start Sequential Recommendation via Meta Learner

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

items.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning, generalization, and simulating human-like behavior across a wide range of tasks. These strengths present new opportunities to enhance traditional recommendation systems (RS), especially in the cold-start item scenario where newly introduced items lack interactions. Existing works have used LLMs to address cold-start issues in traditional RS through data augmentation, but they have limitations. One recent work directly addresses this issue by prompting LLMs to generate augmented interaction data between randomly sampled users and cold-start items. Then, they train the traditional RS with augmented data, incorporating collaborative signals for cold-start items. Although they use LLMs to provide cold-start items with feedback, they use partial user histories, which does not allow the LLM to fully emulate the user. Furthermore, randomly selecting users is not optimal for augmentation. To address these challenges, we leverage the LLM as a user and develop a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that trains a policy to select users for augmentation, optimizing for cold-start item performance after augmented training. The policy model learns to select users for cold-start item data augmentation based on their behavioral features and histories. To optimize user selection for cold-start item performance, we employ a policy gradient method that updates the policy in the direction of actions that lead to high rewards. Experiments on Amazon Product Review datasets show substantial gains in cold-start item recall, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method as a scalable, serving-efficient augmentation strategy for modern RS.
items.title: Selecting User Histories to Generate LLM Users for Cold-Start Item Recommendation

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

items.summary: When a new user just signs up on a website, we usually have no information about him/her, i.e. no interaction with items, no user profile and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, we still expect our recommender systems could attract the users at the first time so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls into new user cold-start category and it is crucial to the development and even survival of a company. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, e.g. setting up an interview process, or make use of side information [10] such as user demographics, locations, social relations, etc. However, users may not be willing to take the interview and side information on cold-start users is usually not available. Therefore, we consider a pure cold-start scenario where neither interaction nor side information is available and no user effort is required. Studying this setting is also important for the initialization of other cold-start solutions, such as initializing the first few questions of an interview.
items.title: The item selection problem for user cold-start recommendation

metadata.query_text: content-based sequential recommendation cold-start item attributes user profile feature quality noise impact

items.summary: Federated recommendation system usually trains a global model on the server without direct access to users' private data on their own devices. However, this separation of the recommendation model and users' private data poses a challenge in providing quality service, particularly when it comes to new items, namely cold-start recommendations in federated settings. This paper introduces a novel method called Item-aligned Federated Aggregation (IFedRec) to address this challenge. It is the first research work in federated recommendation to specifically study the cold-start scenario. The proposed method learns two sets of item representations by leveraging item attributes and interaction records simultaneously. Additionally, an item representation alignment mechanism is designed to align two item representations and learn the meta attribute network at the server within a federated learning framework. Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate IFedRec's superior performance for cold-start scenarios. Furthermore, we also verify IFedRec owns good robustness when the system faces limited client participation and noise injection, which brings promising practical application potential in privacy-protection enhanced federated recommendation systems. The implementation code is available
items.title: When Federated Recommendation Meets Cold-Start Problem: Separating Item Attributes and User Interactions

metadata.query_text: cold-start recommendation content information effectiveness missing data sparsity

items.summary: Multimedia recommendation aims to fuse the multi-modal information of items for feature enrichment to improve the recommendation performance. However, existing methods typically introduce multi-modal information based on collaborative information to improve the overall recommendation precision, while failing to explore its cold-start recommendation performance. Meanwhile, these above methods are only applicable when such multi-modal data is available. To address this problem, this paper proposes a recommendation framework, named Cross-modal Content Inference and Feature Enrichment Recommendation (CIERec), which exploits the multi-modal information to improve its cold-start recommendation performance. Specifically, CIERec first introduces image annotation as the privileged information to help guide the mapping of unified features from the visual space to the semantic space in the training phase. And then CIERec enriches the content representation with the fusion of collaborative, visual, and cross-modal inferred representations, so as to improve its cold-start recommendation performance. Experimental results on two real-world datasets show that the content representations learned by CIERec are able to achieve superior cold-start recommendation performance over existing visually-aware recommendation algorithms. More importantly, CIERec can consistently achieve significant improvements with different conventional visually-aware backbones, which verifies its universality and effectiveness.
items.title: Cross-Modal Content Inference and Feature Enrichment for Cold-Start Recommendation

ablations.expected_observation: recover the unchanged frozen baseline path
ablations.name: module-off parity

ablations.change: enable only content_aware_coldstart_adapter under the matched budget
ablations.expected_observation: The adapter should reduce the performance gap between cold-start and non-cold-start scenarios, particularly improving ranking metrics for users with fewer than 5 interactions and items with no prior interactions. It should also enhance recommendation diversity by surfacing content-similar items that are not in the user's interaction history.
ablations.name: single-module causal test

baseline.description: Accepted evidence constrains baseline selection; implementation version, reproduction, and deployment fit remain pilot gates.
baseline.name: Cold-start Sequential Recommendation via Meta Learner

method.falsifiable_hypothesis: Under The base sequential recommendation model is already trained and frozen, with a fixed architecture and hyperparameters. The adapter is initialized with a pre-trained language-model prior for item similarity estimation., Add a content-aware adapter module to the sequential recommendation pipeline. The module consists of a lightweight transformer encoder for content feature fusion and a similarity estimation head to compute item-item and user-item relevance scores. The adapter is trained jointly with the base model using a weighted combination of standard sequential loss and a content-aware prior loss. should Expected improvement in ranking metrics (e.g., NDCG, HR@K) for cold-start users and items, with minimal degradation in non-cold-start scenarios. The adapter should particularly benefit serendipity and coverage metrics by reducing over-reliance on interaction history. because The adapter injects a content-aware prior into the sequential model's scoring function by combining language-model-derived item similarities with user preference estimates derived from content features. This prior acts as a regularizer during training and inference, guiding the model to focus on relevant items even when interaction signals are weak or missing. addresses Performance heavily depends on the quality and richness of content features; noisy or sparse metadata may degrade recommendation quality. The adapter adds minimal computational overhead but requires content feature extraction at inference time., while The adapter must not introduce leakage between training and test splits. Content features used for prior estimation must be derived solely from metadata available at inference time, and the language-model prior must be pre-trained on a separate corpus to avoid data contamination..

integration_contracts.input: User interaction sequence (item IDs and timestamps), item attributes (e.g., category, text, images), user profile (e.g., demographics, preferences), and contextual metadata (e.g., time, device).
integration_contracts.output: Adjusted recommendation scores for candidate items, incorporating the content-aware prior. The output is a ranked list of items for each user, with improved performance for cold-start users and items.

baseline.dataset: unresolved task-matched data source; no public dataset is required at discovery time; use task-appropriate user-owned, synthetic, simulated, or newly collected data and freeze provenance, split, license, and fingerprint before execution

baseline.name: Cold-start Sequential Recommendation via Meta Learner
baseline.seed_policy: three fixed seeds (1, 2, 3) for all pilot comparisons
baseline.split: not yet frozen; preserve the documented benchmark split and record data hashes
baseline.version_or_commit: inferred from evidence relation at arxiv:2012.05462; reproduce and freeze an implementation before module integration

limitations: Evidence primarily focuses on item cold-start; explicit treatment of user cold-start is limited to a subset of papers (e.g., GS2-RS, Mecos).
Most methods assume availability of structured or high-quality content features, which may not hold in real-world scenarios with noisy or sparse metadata.
Content-based methods may suffer from feature noise or bias, especially when content features are sparse or low-quality.
Hybrid approaches often rely on alignment with collaborative filtering embeddings, which may inherit biases from interaction data.
Performance improvements are dataset-dependent; generalization to unseen domains or long-tail items is not fully addressed.
Some methods (e.g., federated aggregation) introduce additional complexity and computational overhead, which may limit practical deployment.
supported_claims: Content-based and hybrid methods can effectively address item cold-start in recommendation systems by leveraging item attributes or multimodal features.
Purely content-based modeling for cold-start items, without alignment with collaborative filtering embeddings, can achieve competitive ranking accuracy.
evidence.title: Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation

hypothesis.condition: The base sequential recommendation model is already trained and frozen, with a fixed architecture and hyperparameters. The adapter is initialized with a pre-trained language-model prior for item similarity estimation.
hypothesis.guardrail: The adapter must not introduce leakage between training and test splits. Content features used for prior estimation must be derived solely from metadata available at inference time, and the language-model prior must be pre-trained on a separate corpus to avoid data contamination.
hypothesis.intervention: Add a content-aware adapter module to the sequential recommendation pipeline. The module consists of a lightweight transformer encoder for content feature fusion and a similarity estimation head to compute item-item and user-item relevance scores. The adapter is trained jointly with the base model using a weighted combination of standard sequential loss and a content-aware prior loss.
hypothesis.limitation: Performance heavily depends on the quality and richness of content features; noisy or sparse metadata may degrade recommendation quality. The adapter adds minimal computational overhead but requires content feature extraction at inference time.
hypothesis.mechanism: The adapter injects a content-aware prior into the sequential model's scoring function by combining language-model-derived item similarities with user preference estimates derived from content features. This prior acts as a regularizer during training and inference, guiding the model to focus on relevant items even when interaction signals are weak or missing.
hypothesis.predicted_metric_change: Expected improvement in ranking metrics (e.g., NDCG, HR@K) for cold-start users and items, with minimal degradation in non-cold-start scenarios. The adapter should particularly benefit serendipity and coverage metrics by reducing over-reliance on interaction history.

It should also enhance recommendation diversity by surfacing content-similar items that are not in the user's interaction history.

research.intended_claim: Introduce a lightweight adapter module that augments sequential recommendation models with a content-aware prior for cold-start users and items. The adapter uses a language-model-derived prior to estimate item similarities and user preferences from content features (e.g., item attributes, user profiles, contextual metadata), enabling the model to generate meaningful recommendations even when interaction data is sparse or absent. The adapter is trained end-to-end with the base sequential model and can be independently switched on/off.

method.problem_method_insight: In sequential recommendation, cold-start scenarios involve both new items (item cold-start) and users with short interaction histories (user cold-start). Existing content-based and hybrid methods primarily address item cold-start by leveraging item attributes or multimodal features, but they often fail to simultaneously handle user cold-start due to the lack of interaction signals. Meta-learning and serendipity-oriented approaches provide partial solutions but are not designed for integrated treatment of both cold-start types in sequential settings.

supporting_spans: Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation.
Item cold-start is a pervasive challenge for collaborative filtering (CF) recommender systems.
Existing methods often train cold-start models by mapping auxiliary item content, such as images or text descriptions, into the embedding space of a CF model.
Language-Model Prior Overcomes Cold-Start Items.
The growth of recommender systems (RecSys) is driven by digitization and the need for personalized content in areas such as e-commerce and video streaming.
The content in these systems often changes rapidly and therefore they constantly face the ongoing cold-start problem, where new items lack interaction data and are hard to value.
General Item Representation Learning for Cold-start Content Recommendations.
Cold-start item recommendation is a long-standing challenge in recommendation systems.
A common remedy is to use a content-based approach, but rich information from raw contents in various forms has not been fully utilized.
Metadata Embeddings for User and Item Cold-start Recommendations.
I present a hybrid matrix factorisation model representing users and items as linear combinations of their content features' latent factors.

The model outperforms both collaborative and content-based models in cold-start or sparse interaction data scenarios (using both user and item metadata), and performs at least as well as a pure collaborative matrix factorisation model where interaction data is abundant.
Generating Self-Serendipity Preference in Recommender Systems for Addressing Cold Start Problems.

Classical accuracy-oriented Recommender Systems (RSs) typically face the cold-start problem and the filter-bubble problem when users suffer the familiar, repeated, and even predictable recommendations, making them boring and unsatisfied.

To address the above issues, serendipity-oriented RSs are proposed to recommend appealing and valuable items significantly deviating from users' historical interactions and thus satisfying them by introducing unexplored but relevant candidate items to them.
Cold-start Sequential Recommendation via Meta Learner.
This paper explores meta-learning in sequential recommendation to alleviate the item cold-start problem.
Sequential recommendation aims to capture user's dynamic preferences based on historical behavior sequences and acts as a key component of most online recommendation scenarios.
Selecting User Histories to Generate LLM Users for Cold-Start Item Recommendation.
These strengths present new opportunities to enhance traditional recommendation systems (RS), especially in the cold-start item scenario where newly introduced items lack interactions.
Existing works have used LLMs to address cold-start issues in traditional RS through data augmentation, but they have limitations.
The item selection problem for user cold-start recommendation.
When a new user just signs up on a website, we usually have no information about him/her, i.e.
no interaction with items, no user profile and no social links with other users.
When Federated Recommendation Meets Cold-Start Problem: Separating Item Attributes and User Interactions.
Federated recommendation system usually trains a global model on the server without direct access to users' private data on their own devices.

However, this separation of the recommendation model and users' private data poses a challenge in providing quality service, particularly when it comes to new items, namely cold-start recommendations in federated settings.
Cross-Modal Content Inference and Feature Enrichment for Cold-Start Recommendation.
Multimedia recommendation aims to fuse the multi-modal information of items for feature enrichment to improve the recommendation performance.

However, existing methods typically introduce multi-modal information based on collaborative information to improve the overall recommendation precision, while failing to explore its cold-start recommendation performance.

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

raw_candidates.snippet: Item cold-start is a pervasive challenge for collaborative filtering (CF) recommender systems. Existing methods often train cold-start models by mapping auxiliary item content, such as images or text descriptions, into the embedding space of a CF model. However, such approaches can be limited by the fundamental information gap between CF signals and content features. In this work, we propose to avoid this limitation with purely content-based modeling of cold items, i.e. without alignment with CF user or item embeddings. We instead frame cold-start prediction in terms of item-item similarity, training a content encoder to project into a latent space where similarity correlates with user preferences. We define our training objective as a sparse generalization of sampled softmax loss with the $α$-entmax family of activation functions, which allows for sharper estimation of item relevance by zeroing gradients for uninformative negatives. We then describe how this Sampled Entmax for Cold-start (SEMCo) training regime can be extended via knowledge distillation, and show that it outperforms existing cold-start methods and standard sampled softmax in ranking accuracy. We also discuss the advantages of purely content-based modeling, particularly in terms of equity of item outcomes.
raw_candidates.title: Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

raw_candidates.snippet: The growth of recommender systems (RecSys) is driven by digitization and the need for personalized content in areas such as e-commerce and video streaming. The content in these systems often changes rapidly and therefore they constantly face the ongoing cold-start problem, where new items lack interaction data and are hard to value. Existing solutions for the cold-start problem, such as content-based recommenders and hybrid methods, leverage item metadata to determine item similarities. The main challenge with these methods is their reliance on structured and informative metadata to capture detailed item similarities, which may not always be available. This paper introduces a novel approach for cold-start item recommendation that utilizes the language model (LM) to estimate item similarities, which are further integrated as a Bayesian prior with classic recommender systems. This approach is generic and able to boost the performance of various recommenders. Specifically, our experiments integrate it with both sequential and collaborative filtering-based recommender and evaluate it on two real-world datasets, demonstrating the enhanced performance of the proposed approach.
raw_candidates.title: Language-Model Prior Overcomes Cold-Start Items

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

raw_candidates.snippet: Cold-start item recommendation is a long-standing challenge in recommendation systems. A common remedy is to use a content-based approach, but rich information from raw contents in various forms has not been fully utilized. In this paper, we propose a domain/data-agnostic item representation learning framework for cold-start recommendations, naturally equipped with multimodal alignment among various features by adopting a Transformer-based architecture. Our proposed model is end-to-end trainable completely free from classification labels, not just costly to collect but suboptimal for recommendation-purpose representation learning. From extensive experiments on real-world movie and news recommendation benchmarks, we verify that our approach better preserves fine-grained user taste than state-of-the-art baselines, universally applicable to multiple domains at large scale.
raw_candidates.title: General Item Representation Learning for Cold-start Content Recommendations

metadata.query_text: sequence recommendation cold-start item user content-based hybrid

raw_candidates.snippet: I present a hybrid matrix factorisation model representing users and items as linear combinations of their content features' latent factors. The model outperforms both collaborative and content-based models in cold-start or sparse interaction data scenarios (using both user and item metadata), and performs at least as well as a pure collaborative matrix factorisation model where interaction data is abundant. Additionally, feature embeddings produced by the model encode semantic information in a way reminiscent of word embedding approaches, making them useful for a range of related tasks such as tag recommendations.
raw_candidates.title: Metadata Embeddings for User and Item Cold-start Recommendations

metadata.query_text: sequential recommendation new user item content information cold start taxonomy

raw_candidates.snippet: Classical accuracy-oriented Recommender Systems (RSs) typically face the cold-start problem and the filter-bubble problem when users suffer the familiar, repeated, and even predictable recommendations, making them boring and unsatisfied. To address the above issues, serendipity-oriented RSs are proposed to recommend appealing and valuable items significantly deviating from users' historical interactions and thus satisfying them by introducing unexplored but relevant candidate items to them. In this paper, we devise a novel serendipity-oriented recommender system (\textbf{G}enerative \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{S}erendipity \textbf{R}ecommender \textbf{S}ystem, \textbf{GS$^2$-RS}) that generates users' self-serendipity preferences to enhance the recommendation performance. Specifically, this model extracts users' interest and satisfaction preferences, generates virtual but convincible neighbors' preferences from themselves, and achieves their self-serendipity preference. Then these preferences are injected into the rating matrix as additional information for RS models. Note that GS$^2$-RS can not only tackle the cold-start problem but also provides diverse but relevant recommendations to relieve the filter-bubble problem. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets illustrate that the proposed GS$^2$-RS model can significantly outperform the state-of-the-art baseline approaches in serendipity measures with a stable accuracy performance.
raw_candidates.title: Generating Self-Serendipity Preference in Recommender Systems for Addressing Cold Start Problems

metadata.query_text: sequential recommendation cold-start baseline dataset reproducible open-source implementation

raw_candidates.snippet: This paper explores meta-learning in sequential recommendation to alleviate the item cold-start problem. Sequential recommendation aims to capture user's dynamic preferences based on historical behavior sequences and acts as a key component of most online recommendation scenarios. However, most previous methods have trouble recommending cold-start items, which are prevalent in those scenarios. As there is generally no side information in the setting of sequential recommendation task, previous cold-start methods could not be applied when only user-item interactions are available. Thus, we propose a Meta-learning-based Cold-Start Sequential Recommendation Framework, namely Mecos, to mitigate the item cold-start problem in sequential recommendation. This task is non-trivial as it targets at an important problem in a novel and challenging context. Mecos effectively extracts user preference from limited interactions and learns to match the target cold-start item with the potential user. Besides, our framework can be painlessly integrated with neural network-based models. Extensive experiments conducted on three real-world datasets verify the superiority of Mecos, with the average improvement up to 99%, 91%, and 70% in HR@10 over state-of-the-art baseline methods.
raw_candidates.title: Cold-start Sequential Recommendation via Meta Learner

metadata.query_text: sequential recommendation cold-start baseline dataset reproducible open-source implementation

raw_candidates.snippet: The growth of recommender systems (RecSys) is driven by digitization and the need for personalized content in areas such as e-commerce and video streaming. The content in these systems often changes rapidly and therefore they constantly face the ongoing cold-start problem, where new items lack interaction data and are hard to value. Existing solutions for the cold-start problem, such as content-based recommenders and hybrid methods, leverage item metadata to determine item similarities. The main challenge with these methods is their reliance on structured and informative metadata to capture detailed item similarities, which may not always be available. This paper introduces a novel approach for cold-start item recommendation that utilizes the language model (LM) to estimate item similarities, which are further integrated as a Bayesian prior with classic recommender systems. This approach is generic and able to boost the performance of various recommenders. Specifically, our experiments integrate it with both sequential and collaborative filtering-based recommender and evaluate it on two real-world datasets, demonstrating the enhanced performance of the proposed approach.
raw_candidates.title: Language-Model Prior Overcomes Cold-Start Items

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

raw_candidates.snippet: Cold-start item recommendation is a long-standing challenge in recommendation systems. A common remedy is to use a content-based approach, but rich information from raw contents in various forms has not been fully utilized. In this paper, we propose a domain/data-agnostic item representation learning framework for cold-start recommendations, naturally equipped with multimodal alignment among various features by adopting a Transformer-based architecture. Our proposed model is end-to-end trainable completely free from classification labels, not just costly to collect but suboptimal for recommendation-purpose representation learning. From extensive experiments on real-world movie and news recommendation benchmarks, we verify that our approach better preserves fine-grained user taste than state-of-the-art baselines, universally applicable to multiple domains at large scale.
raw_candidates.title: General Item Representation Learning for Cold-start Content Recommendations

metadata.query_text: sequence-aware recommendation item cold-start user content features

raw_candidates.snippet: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning, generalization, and simulating human-like behavior across a wide range of tasks. These strengths present new opportunities to enhance traditional recommendation systems (RS), especially in the cold-start item scenario where newly introduced items lack interactions. Existing works have used LLMs to address cold-start issues in traditional RS through data augmentation, but they have limitations. One recent work directly addresses this issue by prompting LLMs to generate augmented interaction data between randomly sampled users and cold-start items. Then, they train the traditional RS with augmented data, incorporating collaborative signals for cold-start items. Although they use LLMs to provide cold-start items with feedback, they use partial user histories, which does not allow the LLM to fully emulate the user. Furthermore, randomly selecting users is not optimal for augmentation. To address these challenges, we leverage the LLM as a user and develop a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that trains a policy to select users for augmentation, optimizing for cold-start item performance after augmented training. The policy model learns to select users for cold-start item data augmentation based on their behavioral features and histories. To optimize user selection for cold-start item performance, we employ a policy gradient method that updates the policy in the direction of actions that lead to high rewards. Experiments on Amazon Product Review datasets show substantial gains in cold-start item recall, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method as a scalable, serving-efficient augmentation strategy for modern RS.
raw_candidates.title: Selecting User Histories to Generate LLM Users for Cold-Start Item Recommendation

raw_candidates.snippet: The growth of recommender systems (RecSys) is driven by digitization and the need for personalized content in areas such as e-commerce and video streaming. The content in these systems often changes rapidly and therefore they constantly face the ongoing cold-start problem, where new items lack interaction data and are hard to value. Existing solutions for the cold-start problem, such as content-based recommenders and hybrid methods, leverage item metadata to determine item similarities. The main challenge with these methods is their reliance on structured and informative metadata to capture detailed item similarities, which may not always be available. This paper introduces a novel approach for cold-start item recommendation that utilizes the language model (LM) to estimate item similarities, which are further integrated as a Bayesian prior with classic recommender systems. This approach is generic and able to boost the performance of various recommenders. Specifically, our experiments integrate it with both sequential and collaborative filtering-based recommender and evaluate it on two real-world datasets, demonstrating the enhanced performance of the proposed approach.
