raw_candidates.snippet: When a new user just signs up on a website, we usually have no information about him/her, i.e. no interaction with items, no user profile and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, we still expect our recommender systems could attract the users at the first time so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls into new user cold-start category and it is crucial to the development and even survival of a company. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, e.g. setting up an interview process, or make use of side information [10] such as user demographics, locations, social relations, etc. However, users may not be willing to take the interview and side information on cold-start users is usually not available. Therefore, we consider a pure cold-start scenario where neither interaction nor side information is available and no user effort is required. Studying this setting is also important for the initialization of other cold-start solutions, such as initializing the first few questions of an interview.
raw_candidates.title: The item selection problem for user cold-start recommendation

metadata.query_text: content-based sequential recommendation cold-start item attributes user profile feature quality noise impact

raw_candidates.snippet: Federated recommendation system usually trains a global model on the server without direct access to users' private data on their own devices. However, this separation of the recommendation model and users' private data poses a challenge in providing quality service, particularly when it comes to new items, namely cold-start recommendations in federated settings. This paper introduces a novel method called Item-aligned Federated Aggregation (IFedRec) to address this challenge. It is the first research work in federated recommendation to specifically study the cold-start scenario. The proposed method learns two sets of item representations by leveraging item attributes and interaction records simultaneously. Additionally, an item representation alignment mechanism is designed to align two item representations and learn the meta attribute network at the server within a federated learning framework. Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate IFedRec's superior performance for cold-start scenarios. Furthermore, we also verify IFedRec owns good robustness when the system faces limited client participation and noise injection, which brings promising practical application potential in privacy-protection enhanced federated recommendation systems. The implementation code is available
raw_candidates.title: When Federated Recommendation Meets Cold-Start Problem: Separating Item Attributes and User Interactions

metadata.query_text: cold-start recommendation content information effectiveness missing data sparsity

raw_candidates.snippet: Multimedia recommendation aims to fuse the multi-modal information of items for feature enrichment to improve the recommendation performance. However, existing methods typically introduce multi-modal information based on collaborative information to improve the overall recommendation precision, while failing to explore its cold-start recommendation performance. Meanwhile, these above methods are only applicable when such multi-modal data is available. To address this problem, this paper proposes a recommendation framework, named Cross-modal Content Inference and Feature Enrichment Recommendation (CIERec), which exploits the multi-modal information to improve its cold-start recommendation performance. Specifically, CIERec first introduces image annotation as the privileged information to help guide the mapping of unified features from the visual space to the semantic space in the training phase. And then CIERec enriches the content representation with the fusion of collaborative, visual, and cross-modal inferred representations, so as to improve its cold-start recommendation performance. Experimental results on two real-world datasets show that the content representations learned by CIERec are able to achieve superior cold-start recommendation performance over existing visually-aware recommendation algorithms. More importantly, CIERec can consistently achieve significant improvements with different conventional visually-aware backbones, which verifies its universality and effectiveness.
raw_candidates.title: Cross-Modal Content Inference and Feature Enrichment for Cold-Start Recommendation

limitations: Evidence primarily focuses on item cold-start; explicit treatment of user cold-start is limited to a subset of papers (e.g., GS2-RS, Mecos).
Most methods assume availability of structured or high-quality content features, which may not hold in real-world scenarios with noisy or sparse metadata.
gap_assessments.summary: All accepted evidence directly addresses the item cold-start problem in recommendation systems, proposing content-based, hybrid, or meta-learning approaches to leverage item attributes or multimodal features for improving cold-start performance.

limitations: Limited number of papers directly addressing user cold-start; most solutions are evaluated in specific contexts (e.g., serendipity, sequential recommendation).
Lack of standardized benchmarks or datasets specifically designed for user cold-start evaluation.
gap_assessments.summary: A minority of accepted evidence explicitly targets user cold-start scenarios, often in combination with item cold-start. GS2-RS and Mecos propose serendipity-oriented and meta-learning approaches to address user cold-start, but broader applicability and scalability remain underexplored.

limitations: Content-based methods may suffer from feature noise or bias, especially when content features are sparse or low-quality.
Hybrid approaches often rely on alignment with collaborative filtering embeddings, which may inherit biases from interaction data.
gap_assessments.summary: All accepted evidence supports the use of content-based or hybrid methods to improve cold-start recommendation performance, particularly in item cold-start scenarios. Techniques include content embeddings, multimodal alignment, language-model priors, and federated aggregation.

limitations: Performance improvements are dataset-dependent; generalization to unseen domains or long-tail items is not fully addressed.
Some methods (e.g., federated aggregation) introduce additional complexity and computational overhead, which may limit practical deployment.
gap_assessments.summary: All accepted evidence demonstrates improved cold-start recommendation performance through content-based, hybrid, or meta-learning methods, with evaluations on real-world datasets or benchmarks. Techniques such as sparse contrastive learning, language-model priors, and federated aggregation show consistent gains over baselines.

limitations: Limited explicit treatment of user cold-start in most evidence; solutions are often intertwined with item cold-start scenarios.
Assumption of high-quality or structured content features may not hold in practice, limiting applicability of content-based methods.
Performance gains are dataset-dependent; generalization to unseen domains or long-tail items is not fully validated.
Some methods (e.g., federated aggregation) introduce additional complexity, which may hinder real-world deployment.
Lack of standardized benchmarks specifically designed for user cold-start evaluation.

verified_findings.text: Content-based and hybrid methods can effectively address item cold-start in recommendation systems by leveraging item attributes or multimodal features.
Purely content-based modeling for cold-start items, without alignment with collaborative filtering embeddings, can achieve competitive ranking accuracy.
Language-model priors can be used to estimate item similarities and improve cold-start recommendation performance when integrated with classic recommender systems.

Meta-learning frameworks (e.g., Mecos) can mitigate item cold-start in sequential recommendation by extracting user preferences from limited interactions and matching cold-start items to potential users.
Serendipity-oriented approaches (e.g., GS2-RS) can address user cold-start by generating virtual preferences to enhance recommendation diversity and performance.
Federated recommendation systems can improve cold-start performance by aligning item attributes and interaction records through item representation learning.
Multimodal content inference (e.g., CIERec) can enrich item representations and improve cold-start recommendation performance by fusing visual and semantic features.
